name: Reduced-Sugar Vanilla Donut Glaze
description: A pure sweet white glaze for protein timbits/donut holes - the classic thin sugar glaze that actually crusts. Uses real granulated sugar at HALF the usual amount (blitzed with a little cornstarch into DIY powdered sugar), with liquid monk fruit carrying the rest of the sweetness - so it sets to a true Tim-Hortons-style skin (sucrose recrystallizing as it air-dries) while staying light: fat-free carbs, about half the sugar of a full glaze. Flavor-neutral on purpose so it tops any timbit flavor. Reusable sub-recipe; 1 batch glazes ~16 timbits. Dip warm, air-dry on a rack (never the fridge), double-dip for a thicker shell.

steps.instructionText: Blitz the granulated sugar with the cornstarch to a fine powder in a blender or spice grinder - this is DIY powdered sugar. The cornstarch keeps it from caking and gives the glaze body; sift out any grit.

Whisk the powdered sugar with just enough warm milk to make a THICK, barely-pourable glaze - start with 1 tbsp and add by the teaspoon; a thin glaze won't crust. Whisk in the liquid monk fruit, vanilla, and a pinch of salt. For the best set, warm it ~10-15 sec and stir until the sugar is fully dissolved and smooth - dissolved sugar re-crystallizes into a cleaner crust as it dries. Taste and add monk fruit a drop at a time for sweetness.

notes.text: This is a real (half-quantity) sugar glaze, so it actually crusts like a donut-shop glaze - but only if you dry it right: dip the timbits warm, then AIR-DRY on a rack at room temp. Do NOT refrigerate it - cold and damp keep it tacky and kill the crust. It skins over in ~15-20 min; for a thicker Tim-Hortons-style shell, double-dip (glaze, let set, glaze again).

Half the sugar of a full glaze, with liquid monk fruit carrying the rest of the sweetness - that keeps it light (fat-free carbs, ~half the sugar) while still giving a real sugar crust. Don't replace the missing sugar with granulated Splenda: it's maltodextrin-based and hygroscopic, which actively fights the crust you're after. Sugar sets; monk fruit sweetens.

Flavor-neutral on purpose. To theme it to a flavor without breaking the all-timbit reusability, swap the vanilla for another extract (maple, almond, peppermint) - but for chocolate timbits leave it plain vanilla so the glaze stays the clean sweet contrast to the chocolate.

White granulated sugar gives the cleanest clear-white TH look. Brown sugar works at the same weight but tints the glaze tan and adds a light caramel note - not classic, but honestly nice on a chocolate timbit. Powder it the same way (it may clump from the molasses - sift well).
